did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to classify skin cancer. Specifically, it employs pre-trained models (Inception-V3, MobileNet, VGG-16) using transfer learning and a custom CNN model from scratch to detect and classify skin lesions as benign or malignant.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 2023 International Conference on Sustainable Emerging Innovations in Engineering and Technology (ICSEIET). It describes original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (VGG-16, MobileNet, Inception-V3, and a custom CNN) for skin cancer classification. The authors report specific results, including accuracy metrics (e.g., ~86% average accuracy) and performance measures (precision, recall, F1-score) on a dataset of 3298 images, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: Analysis and Classification of Skin Cancer Using Deep Learning Approach
journal: 2023 International Conference on Sustainable Emerging Innovations in Engineering and Technology (ICSEIET)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning approach to detect and classify skin lesions as benign or malignant using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The authors employed pre-trained models (Inception-V3, MobileNet, VGG-16) via transfer learning and developed a custom CNN from scratch. The models were trained and evaluated on a dataset comprising approximately 2,638 images of benign and cancerous skin infections. Performance was assessed using metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score, with the custom CNN achieving an accuracy of 83.48%. The research also includes a web application interface for practical deployment of the diagnostic tool.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as class 0 in the binary classification task. The dataset contained approximately equal numbers of benign and cancerous images (total 3298 images, sourced from Kaggle).

Classified as class 1 in the binary classification task. The study focuses on detecting and classifying skin cancer, specifically mentioning melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma in the introduction and conclusion as types of skin cancer, though the experimental dataset was labeled broadly as 'benign' vs 'cancerous'.

Mentioned in the introduction as a deadly form of skin cancer and in the conclusion as a type of skin lesion CNNs can classify. However, the specific experimental dataset used in the study was not explicitly described as containing only melanoma or specific histological subtypes, but rather a general 'benign vs cancerous' binary split.

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the deadliest varieties of skin cancer and in the conclusion as a type of skin lesion. Not explicitly isolated as a distinct class in the reported experimental results.

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the deadliest varieties of skin cancer and in the conclusion as a type of skin lesion. Not explicitly isolated as a distinct class in the reported experimental results.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify different categories of images' and 'classify lesions either as benign or malignant.' The task is described as a 'binary task' with outputs denoted as [0,1] for normal/benign or malignant/cancerous skin cells. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from Kaggle containing images of benign and cancerous skin infections/lesions.

use.training: Used to train the pre-trained models (Inception-V3, MobileNet, VGG-16) via transfer learning and the custom CNN model from scratch.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

MobileNet.notes: Outscored other deployed models. PRF scores were 0.86 sequentially.

VGG-16.notes: Trained for 20 periods with learning rate 1e-5. Accuracy curves shown in Fig 2. Specific metric values not explicitly listed in text, but included in comparative analysis.

Inception-V3.notes: Included in comparative analysis. Specific metric values not explicitly listed in text, but included in Table II and III.

Custom CNN.notes: Accuracy was highest around the end of the 20th epoch. Developed from scratch using Keras and TensorFlow.

aggregate_results.notes: The 3 pre-trained baseline models displayed very high accuracy with an estimated average of 86% on the testing set.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple models: VGG-16, MobileNet, Inception-V3 (pre-trained), and a Custom CNN from scratch.
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16, MobileNet, and Inception-V3 were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. For transfer learning, fully connected layers were removed/frozen, and remaining layers were frozen or fine-tuned.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (dataset size 3298 images mentioned).

VGG-16.layers: 16 layers (base), with 3 new dense layers added for binary classification.
VGG-16.activation: ReLU for hidden layers, Sigmoid for output nodes.

MobileNet.description: Designed for mobile/low-power devices. Fully connected layers substituted with additional fully linked layers.

Inception-V3.components: Stacking of inception components with parallel strings of various kernel sizes. Includes average global pooling layer. Batch normalization used. 7x7 convolution layers parameterized with two 3x3 convolutional layers.

Custom CNN.type: CNN built from scratch using Keras and TensorFlow.
Custom CNN.structure:
Comprises two VGG blocks and two Inception blocks. 
- VGG Block: Conv2D (3x3) with max-pooling (stride 2). 
- Inception Block: Conv2D layers with (1,1), (3,3), (5,5), and (3,3) filters. 
- Final layers: Flattening layer, two dense layers (512 and 100 neurons), and one output layer (1 neuron).
Custom CNN.filters: 64 filters in initial VGG block; 64, 96, 128, 32, 16, 32 filters in Inception block.

optimizer.Inception-V3: RMSprop (for retraining variables), Adam (overall optimization mentioned in custom section context, but RMSprop specified for Inception variable updates)

regularization.VGG-16: Dropout (levels not specified in text, but mentioned as 'using dropout levels').
regularization.MobileNet: Early stoppage and LR lowering on peak.
regularization.Inception-V3: Early stoppage and LR lowering on peak.
regularization.Custom CNN: Dropout layers with 0.2 dropout rate, Early stoppage, and LR lowering on peak.

preprocessing_techniques: Transfer learning: Fully connected layers of pre-trained models (VGG-16, MobileNet, Inception-V3) were removed, and remaining layers were frozen or fine-tuned.

Data augmentation/Handling: The study notes that the dataset size (3298 images) is small, which may cause fitting problems, but does not explicitly list specific augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping) applied to the images.
conclusions: The study concludes that deep learning approaches, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), are effective for the automatic detection and classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant.
Among the tested models, MobileNet achieved the highest testing accuracy of 86.06%, outperforming VGG-16, Inception-V3, and the custom CNN model.
The three pre-trained baseline models (VGG-16, MobileNet, Inception-V3) demonstrated very high accuracy with an estimated average of 86% on the testing set.
The custom CNN model developed from scratch achieved a testing accuracy of 83.48%.
Transfer learning is identified as an effective and reliable strategy for handling small datasets (3298 images) where retraining from scratch might cause fitting problems.
The proposed deep learning method offers a quicker and more effective diagnostic alternative to traditional laborious procedures, potentially aiding in the early diagnosis of skin cancer.
A web application interface was developed and demonstrated to provide practical deployment of the diagnostic tool, allowing users to upload images for classification.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small dataset of only 3,298 images sourced from Kaggle, which the authors acknowledge may cause fitting problems and limits the robustness of the results.

The paper does not report specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) for the VGG-16 and Inception-V3 models, only providing detailed results for MobileNet and the custom CNN.
There is no mention of data augmentation techniques applied to the images, despite the small dataset size, which is a significant risk for overfitting in deep learning.
The dataset labels are broadly categorized as 'benign' vs 'cancerous' rather than specific histological subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma), limiting clinical granularity.
The paper lacks external validation; models were trained and tested on a single public dataset without cross-validation or independent test sets from different institutions.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance differences between the models.
The custom CNN model was developed from scratch on the same small dataset, raising concerns about generalizability compared to the pre-trained models.
The web application deployment is demonstrated but lacks details on real-world clinical integration, user validation, or regulatory compliance.
